skills. Now, that's how you build skills
and use them. Let's talk for just a
second about when to use instruction
files versus prompt files versus custom
agents versus skills. So, admittedly,
agents are new and we're sort of still
figuring out where everything fits
together. We've come up with a lot of
different ways of passing instructions
to models and some of this stuff was
invented before skills and skills take
the place of some of those things. But I
think generally speaking, you can think
about it like this. If you have
instructions that you need to pass with
every single prompt to the model, like
general information about the project,
that should be an instructions file. If
you have a short prompt that you find
yourself reusing a lot, that should be a
prompt file. If you want to tweak the
way that the agent behaves, in other
words, you want to define specific
workflows that you want it to follow
every single time, that's a custom
agent. Everything else is probably a
skill. But again, remember that all of
these are just building blocks to help
you build out workflows that work for
you and your team. There isn't really a
right or wrong way to use these things.
They're just different tools that you
have in your toolbox to build out the
optimal workflow. That's the new agent
skills support in Visual Studio Code.
It's available today in VS Code Insiders
and Stable. Make sure that you look for

away. Thanks for watching. And as
